metric_expr: rate(serverStatus.metrics.operation.scanAndOrder.total, 5m) == 0 AND (serverStatus.opLatencies.reads.latency / max(serverStatus.opLatencies.reads.ops,1) > baseline_read_latency * 1.5 OR serverStatus.opLatencies.writes.latency / max(serverStatus.opLatencies.writes.ops,1) > baseline_write_latency * 1.5)
threshold: > baseline by 50% on hosts running AV/EDR without dbPath/logPath exclusions
reason: Scanning MongoDB data and log paths adds unnecessary I/O and CPU overhead because the files are already compressed and may be encrypted. It can also quarantine critical files, causing crashes or corruption.
recommend: Exclude the MongoDB database storage path and log path from antivirus or EDR scanning.
fix:
# Example for Microsoft Defender on Windows
Add-MpPreference -ExclusionPath "C:\data\db","C:\Program Files\MongoDB\Server\log"
# Example for Linux ClamAV (edit config to exclude paths)
echo "ExcludePath ^/var/lib/mongo/" | sudo tee -a /etc/clamav/clamd.conf
echo "ExcludePath ^/var/log/mongodb/" | sudo tee -a /etc/clamav/clamd.conf && sudo systemctl restart clamav-daemon

source.title: MongoDB dbPath
source.quote: If you use an antivirus (AV) scanner or an endpoint detection and response (EDR) scanner, configure your scanner to exclude the database storage path and the database log path from the scan.

reason: The HTTP interface should not be exposed in production. Enabling it increases operational risk and is explicitly discouraged for production deployments.
recommend: Keep the MongoDB HTTP interface disabled in production environments.
fix:
# Ensure no REST/HTTP interface flags are enabled in the service config
sudo sed -i '/rest *=/d;/http *=/d' /etc/mongod.conf
sudo systemctl restart mongod

source.title: Disable HTTP Interface
source.quote: The HTTP interface is disabled by default. Do not enable the HTTP interface in production environments.

reason: Oversized connection pools can overload mongod or mongos connection resources and waste memory and sockets. MongoDB recommends sizing pools close to typical concurrent request volume.
recommend: Set the client connection pool size to about 110-115% of the typical number of current database requests, then tune from there.
fix:
# Example: set MongoDB URI maxPoolSize near 110-115% of typical concurrent requests
export MONGODB_URI='mongodb://db1.example.net/?maxPoolSize=115'
# or update your driver configuration to use maxPoolSize=115

source.title: Manage Connection Pool Sizes
source.quote: Avoid overloading the connection resources of a mongod or mongos instance by adjusting the connection pool size to suit your use case. Start at 110-115% of the typical number of current database requests, and modify the connection pool size as needed.

threshold: uses weak write concern for durability-sensitive writes
reason: Weak write concern returns faster but may acknowledge writes that do not persist through some server failures. This trades durability for latency and can lead to apparent success without persistence.
recommend: Use an appropriate stronger write concern for production durability requirements, such as majority where suitable.

source.title: Write Concern
source.quote: With insufficient write concerns, write operations may appear to a client to have succeeded, but may not persist in some cases of server failure.

reason: Oversizing the WiredTiger internal cache can starve the filesystem cache and other RAM consumers, which can hurt overall performance. MongoDB documentation advises against increasing the cache above its default sizing in most cases.
recommend: Keep WiredTiger cache at the default sizing, or reduce it if filesystem cache or other processes need memory.
fix: db.adminCommand({setParameter:1, wiredTigerEngineRuntimeConfig:'cache_size=DEFAULT'})

source.title: Memory Use
source.quote: Avoid increasing the WiredTiger internal cache size above its default value.

reason: If mongod runs in a container and WiredTiger cache is not sized below the container memory limit, the process can exceed available memory and suffer severe memory pressure or OOM conditions.
recommend: Explicitly set WiredTiger cache size below the RAM available in the container.
fix:
mongod --wiredTigerCacheSizeGB <smaller-than-container-ram-gb>
# or set in config:
# storage:
#   wiredTiger:
#     engineConfig:
#       cacheSizeGB: <value>

source.title: Memory Use
source.quote: If you run mongod in a container (for example, lxc, cgroups, Docker, etc.) that does not have access to all of the RAM available in a system, you must set --wiredTigerCacheSizeGB or --wiredTigerCacheSizePct to a value less than the amount of RAM available in the container.

metric_expr: count(mongod_processes_on_host) > 1 AND serverStatus.wiredTiger.cache['maximum bytes configured'] >= default_wt_cache_bytes_per_instance
reason: The default cache sizing assumes one mongod per machine. On hosts with multiple MongoDB instances, leaving each instance at the default can overcommit RAM and reduce filesystem cache effectiveness.
recommend: Decrease WiredTiger cache size for each mongod instance to leave memory for the other instances and the OS filesystem cache.
fix:
mongod --wiredTigerCacheSizeGB <reduced-per-instance-gb>
# or set in config:
# storage:
#   wiredTiger:
#     engineConfig:
#       cacheSizeGB: <value>

source.title: Memory Use
source.quote: The default WiredTiger internal cache size value assumes that there is a single mongod instance per machine. If a single machine contains multiple MongoDB instances, decrease the setting to accommodate the other mongod instances.

reason: Low available tickets alone is not a reliable overload signal. Queued read or write tickets indicate storage engine transaction concurrency pressure and potential cluster overload.
recommend: Monitor queued read/write tickets as the overload indicator and, if needed, cap or tune concurrent storage engine transactions with storageEngineConcurrentReadTransactions and storageEngineConcurrentWriteTransactions.
fix: db.adminCommand({setParameter:1, storageEngineConcurrentReadTransactions:<n>, storageEngineConcurrentWriteTransactions:<n>})

source.title: Transaction (Read and Write) Concurrency
source.quote: A low value of available in queues.execution does not indicate a cluster overload. Use the number of queued read and write tickets as an indication of cluster overload.

reason: Adding delay to failed authentications can slow brute-force attacks, but it also keeps clients waiting and consuming server resources. Under high connection pressure, this can worsen login latency for legitimate clients.
recommend: Reduce or disable authFailedDelayMs if the server is experiencing connection pressure during many failed logins.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setParameter: 1, authFailedDelayMs: 0 })

source.title: authFailedDelayMs
source.quote: Setting this parameter makes brute-force login attacks on a database more time-consuming. However, clients waiting for a response from the MongoDB server still consume server resources, and this may adversely impact benign login attempts if the server is denying access to many other clients simultaneously.

reason: MongoDB states that concurrent LDAP performance is enabled by ldapForceMultiThreadMode, and that it is required to use the LDAP connection pool. If LDAP auth is in use without this setting, LDAP-related throughput may be limited.
recommend: Enable ldapForceMultiThreadMode together with ldapUseConnectionPool only if your libldap is thread-safe and validated for this mode.
fix: mongod --setParameter ldapForceMultiThreadMode=true --setParameter ldapUseConnectionPool=true

source.title: ldapForceMultiThreadMode
source.quote: Enables the performance of concurrent LDAP operations... You must use ldapForceMultiThreadMode to use LDAP connection pool. To enable LDAP connection pool, set ldapForceMultiThreadMode and ldapUseConnectionPool to true.

threshold: > 0 when ldapRetryCount = 0 and LDAP authorization is enabled
reason: For deployments using LDAP authorization, a zero retry count means the server will not retry LDAP operations after network errors. This can make transient LDAP network issues immediately visible to application authentication/authorization flows.
recommend: Set ldapRetryCount to a small positive value to tolerate transient LDAP network failures.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setParameter: 1, ldapRetryCount: 3 })

source.title: ldapRetryCount
source.quote: For MongoDB deployments using LDAP Authorization on Self-Managed Deployments. Number of operation retries by the server LDAP manager after a network error.

reason: MongoDB 8.0 and later requires Transparent Hugepages to be enabled before mongod starts to benefit from the upgraded TCMalloc behavior and improve resilience under high-stress workloads.
recommend: Enable THP at boot before mongod starts, and set the THP defrag and related kernel values as documented.
fix: sudo systemctl start enable-transparent-huge-pages && sudo systemctl enable enable-transparent-huge-pages

source.title: Enable Transparent Hugepages (THP)
source.quote: In MongoDB 8.0 and later, ensure that THP is enabled before mongod starts by creating a service file for your platform's initialization system.

reason: On RHEL/CentOS, tuned or ktune can override Transparent Hugepages settings, causing the desired THP configuration not to persist for mongod.
recommend: Create and activate a custom tuned profile that keeps transparent_hugepages set to always.
fix:
sudo mkdir -p /etc/tuned/virtual-guest-thp && printf '[main]
include=virtual-guest
[vm]
transparent_hugepages=always
' | sudo tee /etc/tuned/virtual-guest-thp/tuned.conf >/dev/null && sudo tuned-adm profile virtual-guest-thp

source.title: Using tuned and ktune
source.quote: (Optional) Customize tuned or ktune profile

reason: If per-CPU caches are not enabled, TCMalloc falls back to per-thread caches, which increases memory fragmentation relative to the upgraded behavior in MongoDB 8.0.
recommend: Disable glibc rseq before starting mongod so TCMalloc can use rseq and enable per-CPU caches.
fix: export GLIBC_TUNABLES=glibc.pthread.rseq=0

source.title: Disable glibc rseq
source.quote: To ensure that TCMalloc can use rseq to enable per-CPU caches, you can disable glibc’s registration of a rseq structure.

reason: A non-positive cpu_free value indicates per-CPU cache activity is not present as expected, suggesting the upgraded TCMalloc per-CPU cache path is not active.
recommend: Verify per-CPU caches are enabled and, if not, disable glibc rseq and confirm the host kernel is 4.18 or later.
fix: export GLIBC_TUNABLES=glibc.pthread.rseq=0 && uname -r

source.title: Enable Per-CPU Caches
source.quote: To verify that TCMalloc is running with per-CPU caches, ensure that: tcmalloc.usingPerCPUCaches is true. tcmalloc.tcmalloc.cpu_free is greater than 0.

reason: Queries unsupported by current indexes read more documents than they return, which increases latency and wasted I/O. The Performance Advisor explicitly treats suggested indexes as opportunities to improve slow query performance.
recommend: Create an index for frequently executed slow query shapes that show poor query targeting, especially when many documents are scanned per returned document.
fix: db.<collection>.createIndex({ <field1>: 1, <field2>: 1 })

source.title: Common Reasons for Slow Queries
source.quote: If a query is slow, common reasons include: The query is unsupported by your current indexes.

reason: Queries that sort in memory are doing extra work that an appropriate index may avoid. The document lists in-memory sort as a tracked metric for queries that would be improved by a suggested index.
recommend: Add or adjust an index so the query can satisfy both filter and sort from the index rather than sorting in memory.
fix: db.<collection>.createIndex({ <filterField>: 1, <sortField>: 1 })

source.title: Index Metrics
source.quote: In Memory Sort Current number of affected queries per hour that needed to be sorted in memory.

reason: While indexes improve reads, too many indexes increase write overhead because every write must update them. The document advises weighing read benefits against write costs before creating more indexes.
recommend: Review existing indexes and avoid adding new ones unless the slow query is frequent enough and cannot be rewritten to use current indexes.
fix: db.<collection>.dropIndex("<index_name>")

source.title: Index Considerations
source.quote: Indexes improve read performance, but a large number of indexes can negatively impact write performance since indexes must be updated during writes.

reason: Queries using $lookup across multiple collections can be a common cause of slowness because they retrieve information from multiple collections. This often increases work and latency compared with single-collection access patterns.
recommend: Reduce or eliminate expensive $lookup usage where possible, or redesign the query/data model so the access pattern needs fewer cross-collection joins.
fix: db.<collection>.aggregate([ /* rewrite pipeline to remove or reduce $lookup */ ])

source.title: Common Reasons for Slow Queries
source.quote: One query retrieves information from multiple collections with $lookup.

reason: Frequent $lookup usage indicates related data may be split across collections in a way that adds join overhead to slow queries. MongoDB recommends embedding related data when appropriate to reduce these operations.
recommend: Review collections involved in repeated $lookup stages and embed related data that is commonly read together into a single collection where the relationship and document growth make that practical.
fix: # MongoDB schema change; no single command applies. Redesign documents to embed related fields and update application writes/reads accordingly.

source.title: Schema Improvement
source.quote: You are running too many $lookup operations on your data. Take advantage of MongoDB's rich schema model to embed related data in a single collection.

reason: Large unbounded arrays increase document size and can degrade query performance. MongoDB explicitly flags array fields with many elements as a schema issue.
recommend: Bound array growth using patterns such as Subset, Bucket, or Outlier, and move infrequently accessed array elements to separate documents or collections.
fix: # MongoDB schema change; refactor large array fields into bounded arrays or separate documents/collections.

source.title: Schema Improvement
source.quote: Your documents contain array fields with many elements, which can degrade query performance.

reason: Unused or unnecessary indexes consume disk space and add write amplification because each write must maintain them. MongoDB documentation calls out unnecessary indexes as harmful to write performance.
recommend: Identify indexes not used by query patterns and remove them after validating they are not needed for reads, uniqueness, or shard key support.
fix: db.<collection>.dropIndex('<index_name>')

source.title: Schema Improvement
source.quote: You have unnecessary indexes in your collection, which can consume disk space and degrade write performance.

reason: Excessively large documents increase the amount of data read and moved for common operations, hurting frequent-query performance. MongoDB recommends reducing large document size as a schema improvement.
recommend: Shrink document size by splitting rarely used fields into separate documents, duplicating only frequently read subsets, or applying Extended Reference, Subset, or Outlier patterns.
fix: # MongoDB schema change; move infrequently accessed fields out of oversized documents and keep a smaller frequently-read subset.

source.title: Schema Improvement
source.quote: You have excessively large documents, which can degrade the performance of your most frequent queries.

reason: Inefficient regex matching can lead to slow queries when indexes are not effectively used. MongoDB advises using MongoDB Search for these query patterns instead of regex-based scans.
recommend: Replace expensive regex predicates with MongoDB Search queries using the $search aggregation stage where the workload requires text or pattern search.
fix: # Rewrite query to aggregation with $search, e.g. db.<collection>.aggregate([{ $search: { index: '<name>', text: { query: '<term>', path: '<field>' } } }])

source.title: Schema Improvement
source.quote: You are executing queries that rely on inefficient regex matching. Take advantage of MongoDB Search queries that use the $search aggregation pipeline stage.

reason: Quiet mode reduces diagnostic output and can make production issue tracking significantly harder during problematic connections.
recommend: Disable quiet logging in production so operational and connection problems remain visible in logs.
fix:
mongosh --eval 'db.adminCommand({setParameter:1, logLevel:0})' ; perl -0pi -e 's/systemLog:
(.*
)*?\s*quiet:\s*true/systemLog:
  quiet: false/s' /etc/mongod.conf

source.title: systemLog.quiet
source.quote: systemLog.quiet is not recommended for production systems as it may make tracking problems during particular connections much more difficult.

reason: Syslog timestamps are generated by the syslog daemon rather than when MongoDB emits the message, which can mislead troubleshooting under load.
recommend: Use file logging for production systems to preserve accurate MongoDB log timestamps.
fix:
perl -0pi -e "s/destination:\s*syslog/destination: file/" /etc/mongod.conf && grep -q 'systemLog:' /etc/mongod.conf && grep -q 'path:' /etc/mongod.conf || printf '
systemLog:
  destination: file
  path: /var/log/mongodb/mongod.log
  logAppend: true
' >> /etc/mongod.conf && systemctl restart mongod

source.title: systemLog.destination
source.quote: We recommend using the file option for production systems to ensure accurate timestamps.

reason: Using reopen without logAppend violates the documented requirement and can break expected log rotation behavior.
recommend: When using logRotate: reopen, also enable logAppend: true.
fix: perl -0pi -e "s/logRotate:\s*reopen/logRotate: reopen/; if(/systemLog:/s && !/logAppend:/s){s/(logRotate:\s*reopen)/$1\n  logAppend: true/s}else{s/logAppend:\s*false/logAppend: true/s}" /etc/mongod.conf && systemctl restart mongod

source.title: systemLog.logRotate
source.quote: If you specify reopen, you must also set systemLog.logAppend to true.

reason: Higher verbosity levels add Debug messages, increasing log volume and overhead compared with the default informational level.
recommend: Keep global log verbosity at 0 unless actively debugging, and prefer targeted component verbosity when needed.
fix: mongosh --eval 'db.adminCommand({setParameter:1, logLevel:0})'

source.title: systemLog.verbosity
source.quote: 0 is the MongoDB's default log verbosity level, to include Informational messages. 1 to 5 increases the verbosity level to include Debug messages.

reason: If journaling is not active, writes that occur after the last checkpoint may be lost on an unexpected shutdown. MongoDB relies on the journal to recover writes between checkpoints.
recommend: Enable journaling for self-managed mongod instances that need crash recovery of recent writes.
fix: mongod --journal ...

source.title: Configure Journaling for Self-Managed Deployments
source.quote: However, if MongoDB exits unexpectedly in between checkpoints, journaling is required to recover writes that occurred after the last checkpoint.

threshold: journal commit interval effectively allows up to ~100ms of unjournaled writes
reason: MongoDB documents that the default durability window can include writes from the last 100 milliseconds plus journal write time. This defines the potential write-loss exposure during a crash.
recommend: For operations that require commit acknowledgment, use write concern with j enabled so writes are acknowledged only after being committed to the journal.
fix: db.runCommand({setDefaultRWConcern: 1, defaultWriteConcern: {j: true}})

source.title: Get Commit Acknowledgement
source.quote: You can get commit acknowledgment with the Write Concern and the j option.

reason: Journal activity and health should be monitored using the exposed WiredTiger log statistics. Without monitoring, journal stalls or inactivity during write load can go unnoticed.
recommend: Collect and alert on serverStatus().wiredTiger.log metrics as part of MongoDB runtime monitoring.
fix: mongosh --eval 'db.serverStatus().wiredTiger.log'

source.title: Monitor Journal Status
source.quote: The serverStatus command/db.serverStatus() method returns wiredTiger.log, which contains statistics on the journal.

reason: Changing the WiredTiger journal compressor requires a clean shutdown and restart, and an unclean shutdown during the change can require the old compressor settings for recovery. Applying this change incorrectly can delay recovery and availability.
recommend: Change journalCompressor only during a controlled maintenance window with a clean shutdown and restart using the updated setting.
fix: db.getSiblingDB('admin').shutdownServer(); mongod --wiredTigerJournalCompressor <differentCompressor|none> ...

source.title: Change WiredTiger Journal Compressor
source.quote: Perform a clean shutdown of the mongod instance... update --wiredTigerJournalCompressor to the new value during the restart below.

reason: An even number of voting members reduces election efficiency and can require an extra member or arbiter to maintain majority-based failover behavior.
recommend: Configure the replica set with an odd number of voting members.
fix: cfg=rs.conf(); /* adjust member votes or add a voting member/arbiter so total voting members is odd */ rs.reconfig(cfg)

source.title: Deploy an Odd Number of Members
source.quote: Ensure that the replica set has an odd number of voting members.

reason: Multiple arbiters increase operational risk without storing data, and MongoDB explicitly warns against this topology.
recommend: Reduce arbiters to at most one in the replica set.
fix: cfg=rs.conf(); /* remove extra arbiter members */ rs.reconfig(cfg)

source.title: Deploy an Odd Number of Members
source.quote: Avoid deploying more than one arbiter in a replica set.

metric_expr: serverStatus().metrics.commands.find.total - serverStatus().opcounters.query /* combined with application readPreference targeting secondaries */
threshold: > 0 on secondary-directed workload for general reads
reason: MongoDB states that secondaries usually operate under similar load as the primary, so directing reads to secondaries is generally discouraged for performance scaling.
recommend: Do not use secondaries for general read scaling; for read-heavy workloads, replicate to another cluster with Mongosync instead.
fix: // In application connection settings, change readPreference to primary; for dedicated read scaling, deploy Mongosync to a separate cluster

source.title: Read-Heavy Applications
source.quote: You should not direct reads to secondaries. If you have a read-heavy application, consider using Mongosync to replicate data to another cluster for reading.

reason: Disabling journaling increases risk of data loss during power failures or unexpected reboots, which directly affects durability and recovery behavior.
recommend: Keep journaling enabled on replica set members.
fix: mongod --journal  /* or ensure storage.journal.enabled: true in mongod.conf and restart if needed */

source.title: Use Journaling to Protect Against Power Failures
source.quote: MongoDB enables journaling by default. Journaling protects against data loss in the event of service interruptions, such as power failures and unexpected reboots.

reason: Using IP addresses for replica set members can force reconfiguration after IP changes and, in newer versions, IP-only node configuration can fail startup validation.
recommend: Use DNS hostnames instead of IP addresses for replica set member host fields.
fix: cfg=rs.conf(); /* replace each member.host IP with DNS hostname */ rs.reconfig(cfg)

source.title: Hostnames
source.quote: To avoid configuration updates due to IP address changes, use DNS hostnames instead of IP addresses. It is particularly important to use a DNS hostname instead of an IP address when configuring replica set members or sharded cluster members.

metric_expr: count(currentOp.command.createIndexes where currentOp.secs_running > 300 and currentOp.msg contains 'index build')
threshold: > 0 on replica sets while a data-bearing voting member is unreachable
reason: Simultaneous index builds can stall waiting for commit quorum, leaving the build in progress until enough voting members complete. This delays index availability and can prolong resource consumption.
recommend: Restore the unreachable voting member or lower the commit quorum for the in-progress build when appropriate.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setIndexCommitQuorum: "<db>.<collection>", indexNames: ["<index_name>"], commitQuorum: <n_or_mode> })

source.title: Index Builds in Replicated Environments
source.quote: If a data-bearing voting node becomes unreachable and the commitQuorum is set to the default votingMembers, index builds can hang until that node comes back online.

reason: Low free disk space can cause index builds to stop or fail, especially because builds may spill to temporary files on disk. Setting a minimum disk-space guard helps prevent unsafe builds from continuing.
recommend: Set indexBuildMinAvailableDiskSpaceMB to a safe minimum based on expected temporary file usage and available capacity.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setParameter: 1, indexBuildMinAvailableDiskSpaceMB: <mb> })

source.title: Index Builds on Populated Collections
source.quote: You can also set the minimum available disk space required for index builds using the new indexBuildMinAvailableDiskSpaceMB parameter, which stops index builds if disk space is too low.

reason: MongoDB limits concurrent user index builds, and once the limit is reached additional builds are blocked. Tuning this can help control build concurrency and reduce queueing during maintenance windows.
recommend: Adjust maxNumActiveUserIndexBuilds if the default concurrency of three is too low or too high for the host's resources and operational window.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setParameter: 1, maxNumActiveUserIndexBuilds: <n> })

source.title: Maximum Concurrent Index Builds
source.quote: By default, the server allows up to three concurrent index builds. To change the number of allowed concurrent index builds, modify the maxNumActiveUserIndexBuilds parameter.

metric_expr: count(currentOp.command.createIndexes where currentOp.active == true) > 0 AND host.freeMemMB is low relative to maxNumActiveUserIndexBuilds * maxIndexBuildMemoryUsageMegabytes
reason: Index builds use RAM up to the configured per-command limit and then spill to temporary files under _tmp, which can increase I/O and prolong builds. Concurrent builds multiply total memory demand.
recommend: Increase maxIndexBuildMemoryUsageMegabytes only in rare justified cases, or reduce concurrent builds / schedule maintenance when free RAM is limited.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setParameter: 1, maxIndexBuildMemoryUsageMegabytes: <mb> })

source.title: Insufficient Available System Memory (RAM)
source.quote: The default memory limit is 200 megabytes per createIndexes command, shared equally among all indexes built in that command. When you reach the memory limit, MongoDB creates temporary files in the _tmp subdirectory within --dbpath to complete the build.

metric_expr: rate(opcounters.insert + opcounters.update + opcounters.delete) during active createIndexes
threshold: > baseline write-heavy workload while index build is active
reason: Running index builds during heavy write periods reduces write performance and lengthens the build. Scheduling builds during quieter periods lowers operational impact.
recommend: Run index builds in a maintenance window and reduce or stop application writes to the target collection during the build.
fix: db.currentOp()

source.title: Index Builds During Write-Heavy Workloads
source.quote: Building indexes during time periods where the target collection is under heavy write load can result in reduced write performance and longer index builds.

reason: Dropping an index that is actively used can degrade query performance. Hiding the index first lets you test whether queries regress before permanently removing it.
recommend: Hide the index first, observe query performance, and only drop it if there is no substantial negative impact.
fix: db.runCommand({ collMod: "<collection>", index: { name: "<index_name>", hidden: true } })

source.title: Remove Indexes
source.quote: If you drop an index that is actively used in production, your application may incur a performance degradation. Before you drop an index, you can evaluate the potential impact of the drop by hiding the index.

reason: Most index changes require drop-and-recreate, which can remove query support during the change window. A temporary redundant index preserves index coverage and minimizes performance impact while the original index is rebuilt.
recommend: Before modifying a frequently used index, create a temporary redundant index with the same leading fields, then drop and recreate the original index, and finally remove the temporary index.
fix: db.<collection>.createIndex({ "<field>": 1, "dummyField": 1 })

source.title: Minimize Performance Impact With a Temporary Index
source.quote: To ensure queries can still use an index during modification, you can create a temporary, redundant index that contains the same fields as the modified index.

reason: Inconsistent indexes across shards can cause incorrect or uneven query behavior and operational issues. MongoDB explicitly reports the count of sharded collections with index inconsistencies on the config server primary.
recommend: Run metadata consistency checks for indexes on sharded collections and repair missing or mismatched indexes so every shard has the exact same index definitions and options.
fix: db.runCommand({ checkMetadataConsistency: 1, checkIndexes: true })

source.title: Find Inconsistent Indexes Across Shards
source.quote: the command serverStatus, when run on the config server primary, returns the field shardedIndexConsistency field to report on the number of sharded collections with index inconsistencies.

reason: When an index is missing on some shards or has different properties, sharded collections no longer have identical index definitions, which MongoDB identifies as an inconsistency. Rebuilding from mongos or correcting the affected shards restores consistent query support.
recommend: For missing indexes, issue createIndex from mongos so only missing shards build it; for differing properties, drop the incorrect index on affected shards and rebuild it. If only expireAfterSeconds differs, use collMod instead of rebuild.
fix: db.<collection>.createIndex({ "<field>": 1 })

source.title: Find Inconsistent Indexes Across Shards
source.quote: To resolve the inconsistency where an index is missing from the collection on a particular shard(s), You can either: Issue an index build db.collection.createIndex() from a mongos instance. The operation only builds the collection's index on the shard(s) missing the index.

reason: Suppressing most log output reduces observability and can hide performance symptoms needed for diagnosis in production.
recommend: Disable quiet logging for production systems so normal diagnostic information is available.
fix: mongosh --eval 'db.adminCommand({setParameter:1, quiet:false})'

source.title: Configure the Database
source.quote: quiet is true. This disables all but the most critical entries in output/log file, and is not recommended for production systems.

reason: An overly high slow operation threshold can miss actionable slow queries and reduce the usefulness of logs and profiling for performance analysis.
recommend: Lower slowOpThresholdMs to capture more slow queries when current logging and profiler output is not useful.
fix: mongosh --eval 'db.adminCommand({setParameter:1, slowOpThresholdMs:50})'

source.title: Diagnostic Configurations
source.quote: operationProfiling.slowOpThresholdMs configures the threshold which determines whether a query is "slow" for the purpose of the logging system and the profiler. The default value is 100 milliseconds. Set to a lower value if the logging system and the database profiler do not return useful results

reason: Elevated global log verbosity increases logging overhead and should only be used temporarily when investigating issues.
recommend: Return systemLog.verbosity to the normal level after troubleshooting, and avoid raising it unless needed for a specific issue.
fix: mongosh --eval 'db.adminCommand({setParameter:1, logLevel:0})'

source.title: Diagnostic Configurations
source.quote: systemLog.verbosity controls the amount of logging output that mongod write to the log. Only use this option if you are experiencing an issue that is not reflected in the normal logging level.

reason: Running multiple mongod instances on one system can create resource contention and is generally discouraged except for specific deployment types or testing.
recommend: Consolidate to a single mongod per host unless the deployment is explicitly designed for multiple instances and has sufficient fast storage and isolated data paths.
fix: ps -ef | grep '[m]ongod'

source.title: Run Multiple Database Instances on the Same System
source.quote: In many cases running multiple instances of mongod on a single system is not recommended.

reason: MongoDB uses a dynamic algorithm to adjust the maximum number of concurrent transactions, which can optimize throughput but may lead to unexpected behavior if not configured properly.
recommend: Consider setting maximum read and write transactions to ensure they do not exceed 128, especially in high-load scenarios.
fix: Set storageEngineConcurrentReadTransactions and storageEngineConcurrentWriteTransactions to desired maximum values.

source.title: WiredTiger Storage Engine - Database Manual
source.quote: Starting in version 7.0, MongoDB uses a default algorithm to dynamically adjust the maximum number of concurrent storage engine transactions, or read and write tickets. The maximum number of concurrent storage engine transactions, or read and write tickets, never exceeds 128 read tickets and 128 write tickets.

reason: WiredTiger creates checkpoints every 60 seconds to ensure data consistency and durability. This interval is crucial for recovery in case of failures.
recommend: Consider adjusting the checkpoint interval based on your workload requirements.
fix: Set the checkpoint interval in the MongoDB configuration.

source.title: WiredTiger Checkpoints

reason: Enabling Transparent Huge Pages (THP) can improve database performance by allowing the system to manage memory more efficiently, especially for large memory allocations.
recommend: Create an init.d script to enable Transparent Huge Pages at system startup.
fix:
#!/bin/bash
### BEGIN INIT INFO
# Provides: enable-transparent-hugepages
# Required-Start: $local_fs
# Required-Stop:
# X-Start-Before: mongod mongodb-mms-automation-agent
# Default-Start: 2 3 4 5
# Default-Stop: 0 1 6
# Short-Description: Enable Linux Transparent Hugepages
# Description: Enable Linux Transparent Hugepages, to improve database performance.
### END INIT INFO
case $1 in start) if [ -d /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage ]; then thp_path=/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage elif [ -d /sys/kernel/mm/redhat_transparent_hugepage ]; then thp_path=/sys/kernel/mm/redhat_transparent_hugepage else return 0 fi echo 'always' | tee ${thp_path}/enabled > /dev/null && echo defer+madvise | tee ${thp_path}/defrag > /dev/null && echo 0 | tee ${thp_path}/khugepaged/max_ptes_none > /dev/null && echo 1 | tee /proc/sys/vm/overcommit_memory > /dev/null' unset thp_path ;;esac

reason: Placing all replica set members on the same SAN can create a single point of failure, jeopardizing the availability of the database.
recommend: Distribute replica set members across different SANs to enhance fault tolerance.
fix: Configure replica set members to use different SANs or physical drives.

source.title: Hardware
source.quote: Avoid placing all replica set members on the same SAN, as the SAN can be a single point of failure.

reason: The TCP idle timeout on the Azure load balancer is too slow for MongoDB's connection pooling behavior, which can lead to connection issues and degraded performance.
recommend: Adjust the TCP keepalive setting to ensure better connection management in Azure environments.
fix: sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time=100

source.title: Deployments to Cloud Hardware
source.quote: Adjust the TCP keepalive (tcp_keepalive_time) to 100-120. The TCP idle timeout on the Azure load balancer is too slow for MongoDB's connection pooling behavior.

reason: Using an outdated version of MongoDB on high-latency storage can lead to performance issues due to lack of optimizations present in newer versions.
recommend: Upgrade to MongoDB version 2.6.4 or later to benefit from performance improvements on high-latency storage systems.
fix: Upgrade MongoDB to version 2.6.4 or later

source.title: Deployments to Cloud Hardware
source.quote: Use MongoDB version 2.6.4 or later on systems with high-latency storage, such as Windows Azure, as these versions include performance improvements for those systems.

reason: Setting the readahead value appropriately can improve the performance of the WiredTiger storage engine by optimizing how data is read from disk.
recommend: Adjust the readahead settings on the devices storing your database files to be between 8 and 32.
fix: sysctl -w vm.readahead=16

source.title: Deployments to Cloud Hardware
source.quote: For the WiredTiger storage engine, set readahead between 8 and 32 regardless of storage media type (spinning disk, SSD, etc.), unless testing shows a measurable, repeatable, and advice and guidance on alternate readahead configurations.

reason: Swap space management is crucial for performance. If swap is not configured correctly, it can lead to performance degradation, especially under heavy load.
recommend: Ensure that your system has swap space configured or disable swapping entirely based on your workload requirements.
fix: sysctl -w vm.swappiness=0

source.title: Deployments to Cloud Hardware
source.quote: Do not assign swap space on your system, and configure the kernel to disable swapping entirely.

reason: Setting the TCP keepalive time correctly can improve the performance of replica sets and sharded clusters by ensuring that connections are maintained properly.
recommend: Set the default TCP keepalive to 120 seconds for better performance in MongoDB deployments.
fix: sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time=120

source.title: Deployments to Cloud Hardware
source.quote: Ensure that the system default TCP keepalive is set correctly. A value of 120 often provides better performance for replica sets and sharded clusters.

reason: A low 'soft' ulimit can cause 'can't create new thread, closing connection' errors if the number of connections grows too high. This can severely impact the performance and availability of the MongoDB service.
recommend: Set both 'soft' and 'hard' ulimit values to the recommended values to ensure that MongoDB can handle the expected number of connections without issues.
fix: ulimit -n <value>

source.title: UNIX ulimit 配置
source.quote: A low 'soft' ulimit can cause can't create new thread, closing connection errors if the number of connections grows too high. For this reason, it is extremely important to set both ulimit values to the recommended values.

reason: 在 RHEL/CentOS 6 和 7 中，nproc 的最大进程限制会覆盖 ulimit 设置，确保该值足够高以支持 MongoDB 的需求。
recommend: 为 MongoDB 创建一个配置文件以设置适当的 nproc 值。
fix: echo 'soft nproc 4096' >> /etc/security/limits.d/99-mongodb-nproc.conf && echo 'hard nproc 4096' >> /etc/security/limits.d/99-mongodb-nproc.conf

source.title: UNIX ulimit 配置
source.quote: RHEL / CentOS 7 4096 /etc/security/limits.d/20-nproc.conf

reason: 在 RHEL/CentOS 6 中，nproc 的最大进程限制会覆盖 ulimit 设置，确保该值足够高以支持 MongoDB 的需求。
recommend: 为 MongoDB 创建一个配置文件以设置适当的 nproc 值。
fix: echo 'soft nproc 1024' >> /etc/security/limits.d/99-mongodb-nproc.conf && echo 'hard nproc 1024' >> /etc/security/limits.d/99-mongodb-nproc.conf

source.title: UNIX ulimit 配置
source.quote: RHEL / CentOS 6 1024 /etc/security/limits.d/90-nproc.conf

reason: MongoDB 的性能依赖于适当的 ulimit 设置，确保这些设置符合推荐值可以避免潜在的性能问题。
recommend: 根据 MongoDB 的推荐设置调整 ulimit 值。
fix: 参考 MongoDB 官方文档中的推荐 ulimit 设置。

source.title: UNIX ulimit 配置
source.quote: Every deployment may have unique requirements and settings; however, the following thresholds and settings are particularly important for mongod and mongos deployments:

reason: The process limit for incoming connections on macOS is critical to ensure that the MongoDB instance can handle the expected load. If the limit is set too low, it may lead to connection failures and degraded performance.
recommend: Increase the process limit to the recommended value of 2500 to ensure optimal handling of incoming connections.
fix: ulimit -n 2500

source.title: UNIX ulimit 配置
source.quote: For the macOS platform, the recommended process limit is 2500, which is the maximum configurable value for this platform.

reason: Setting appropriate ulimit values is crucial for ensuring that the MongoDB server can handle the expected load without running into resource limits, which can lead to performance degradation or service interruptions.
recommend: Configure the ulimit settings for file size, CPU time, virtual memory size, locked-in-memory size, open files, and processes/threads as recommended.
fix:
limit fsize unlimited unlimited
limit cpu unlimited unlimited
limit as unlimited unlimited
limit memlock unlimited unlimited
limit nofile 64000 64000
limit nproc 64000 64000

source.title: UNIX ulimit 配置
source.quote: Specify the Recommended ulimit Settings, as in the following example: limit fsize unlimited unlimited # (file size) limit cpu unlimited unlimited # (cpu time) limit as unlimited unlimited # (virtual memory size) limit memlock unlimited unlimited # (locked-in-memory size) limit nofile 64000 64000 # (open files) limit nproc 64000 64000 # (processes/threads)

reason: Setting appropriate ulimit values is crucial for ensuring that MongoDB can handle the expected load without running into resource limits, which can lead to service interruptions or degraded performance.
recommend: Ensure that the ulimit settings for open files and processes are set to at least 64000 for MongoDB services running under systemd.
fix: Edit the service file at /etc/systemd/system/<process-name>.service to include 'LimitNOFILE=64000' and 'LimitNPROC=64000'.

source.title: UNIX ulimit 配置
source.quote: You can set limits by using resource limit directives. Specify the Recommended ulimit Settings, as in the following example: LimitNOFILE=64000; LimitNPROC=64000.

reason: Highly selective queries match fewer documents and use indexes more effectively, which can significantly improve query performance.
recommend: Aim to construct highly selective queries, such as using equality matches on indexed fields like _id.
fix: Use equality operators in your queries, e.g., db.collection.find({_id: value}) to ensure high selectivity.

source.title: Create Selective Queries
source.quote: Highly selective queries match fewer documents and use indexes more effectively. For instance, an equality match on _id is highly selective as it can match at most one document.

reason: Less selective queries match more documents and use indexes less efficiently, which can lead to slower query performance.
recommend: Avoid using less selective operators like $nin and $ne when possible, as they may not utilize indexes effectively.
fix: Reformulate queries to use more selective criteria, e.g., instead of using $ne, try to use equality matches where applicable.

source.title: Create Selective Queries
source.quote: Less selective queries match more documents and use indexes less efficiently. For instance, the inequality operators $nin and $ne are not very selective since they often match a large portion of the index.

reason: Returning only the fields you need can significantly reduce network traffic and processing time, improving overall query performance.
recommend: Use projections in your queries to limit the fields returned, e.g., db.posts.find({}, { timestamp: 1, title: 1, author: 1, abstract: 1 }).
fix: db.posts.find({}, { timestamp: 1, title: 1, author: 1, abstract: 1 }).sort({ timestamp: -1 })

source.title: Create Selective Queries
source.quote: When you need a subset of fields from documents, you can improve performance by returning only the fields you need. Projections reduce network traffic and processing time.

reason: Limiting query results helps reduce network resource usage by only retrieving the necessary number of documents.
recommend: Use the limit() method in your queries to specify the maximum number of results you need.
fix: db.collection.find().sort({ field: -1 }).limit(N)

source.title: Limit Query Results
source.quote: If you know how many results you need, pass that value to the limit() method to reduce network resource usage.

reason: Indexing fields within embedded documents allows queries to be covered by the index, improving performance by avoiding the need to read the full documents from disk.
recommend: Use dot notation to create indexes on fields within embedded documents.
fix: db.userdata.createIndex( { 'user.login': 1 } )

source.title: Run Covered Queries
source.quote: An index can cover a query on fields within embedded documents. To index fields in embedded documents, use dot notation.

reason: Stopping writes to a collection before creating an index ensures that no new documents violate the index constraints, preventing potential index inconsistencies.
recommend: Stop all writes to the collection from applications that cannot guarantee violation-free write operations before creating the index.
fix: application.stopWrites()

source.title: Sharded Collections
source.quote: Stop all writes to the collection from applications that cannot guarantee violation-free write operations.

reason: The default commit quorum for index builds is set to 'votingMembers', which includes all data-bearing voting members. This ensures that the index build is committed only when a majority of the members agree, enhancing data consistency.
recommend: Consider specifying a non-default commit quorum if your application requires different consistency or performance characteristics during index builds.
fix: Specify the commitQuorum parameter in the createIndexes command.

source.title: Important
source.quote: By default, index builds use a commit quorum of 'votingMembers', or all data-bearing voting members. To start an index build with a non-default commit quorum, specify the commitQuorum parameter to createIndexes.

reason: Starting in MongoDB 5.0, if a secondary mongod is interrupted during an index build, it can recover from the last checkpoint if the commitQuorum is set correctly. This prevents loss of progress during index builds.
recommend: Ensure that commitQuorum is set to votingMembers to allow recovery of index builds after interruptions.
fix: Set commitQuorum to votingMembers in your index creation command.

source.title: Interrupted Index Builds on a Secondary mongod
source.quote: Starting in MongoDB 5.0, if a secondary mongod performs a clean shutdown with 'force' : true or receives a SIGTERM signal during an index build and the commitQuorum is set to the default votingMembers, then the index build progress is saved to disk.

reason: If the mongod is restarted as a standalone, it cannot recover interrupted index builds, which can lead to data loss and require manual intervention to drop the index.
recommend: Avoid restarting mongod as a standalone if there are ongoing index builds.
fix: Do not remove or comment out replication.replSetName when restarting mongod.

source.title: Interrupted Index Builds on a Secondary mongod
source.quote: If you restart the mongod as a standalone, the mongod cannot restart the index build. The build remains in a paused state until it is manually dropped.

reason: Enabling sharded index consistency checks helps ensure that all shards in a sharded collection have the same indexes, preventing potential issues with inconsistent data access and query performance.
recommend: Enable the sharded index consistency check to maintain index consistency across shards.
fix: setParameter enableShardedIndexConsistencyCheck=true

source.title: Rollbacks during Build Process
source.quote: To configure these periodic checks, see enableShardedIndexConsistencyCheck and shardedIndexConsistencyCheckIntervalMS.

reason: Setting an appropriate interval for sharded index consistency checks can help balance performance and consistency, ensuring that checks are performed regularly without overwhelming the system.
recommend: Configure the sharded index consistency check interval to an appropriate value based on your workload.
fix: setParameter shardedIndexConsistencyCheckIntervalMS=60000

source.title: Rollbacks during Build Process
source.quote: To configure these periodic checks, see enableShardedIndexConsistencyCheck and shardedIndexConsistencyCheckIntervalMS.

reason: Setting a lower slow operation threshold can help identify performance issues more quickly, allowing for timely optimizations. The default threshold of 100 milliseconds may miss slower operations that could impact performance.
recommend: Consider lowering the slow operation threshold to a value like 20 milliseconds to capture more slow operations for profiling.
fix: db.setProfilingLevel(1, { slowms: 20 })

source.title: Specify the Threshold for Slow Operations
source.quote: To change the slow operation threshold, use one of the following: Set the value of slowms using the profile command or db.setProfilingLevel() shell helper method. Set the value of --slowms from the command line at startup. Set the value of slowOpThresholdMs in a configuration file.

reason: Disabling profiling can improve database performance and lower disk use, which is crucial for maintaining optimal resource utilization in production environments.
recommend: Set the profiling level to 0 to disable profiling when not needed, especially in production.
fix: db.setProfilingLevel(0)

source.title: Disable Profiling
source.quote: Disabling profiling can improve database performance and lower disk use.

threshold: has profiling level 1 with slowms 15 and slowOpSampleRate 0.5
reason: Enabling profiling can help identify slow operations, which is useful for performance tuning in development and testing environments.
recommend: Use profiling level 1 to log slow operations for analysis, especially during development.
fix: mongod --profile 1 --slowms 15 --slowOpSampleRate 0.5

source.title: Profiling Levels
source.quote: Pass the following options to mongod at startup: mongod --profile 1 --slowms 15 --slowOpSampleRate 0.5

reason: The size of the system.profile collection can impact the performance of profiling operations. A larger size allows for more profiling data to be stored, which can be useful for performance analysis.
recommend: Consider setting the system.profile collection size to 4 MB to accommodate profiling data without excessive overhead.
fix: Create a new system.profile collection with a size of 4000000 bytes.

source.title: Change Size of system.profile Collection on the Primary
source.quote: For example, to create a new system.profile collection of 4000000 bytes (4 MB) in mongosh.

reason: Running MongoDB 8.0 or newer with Linux kernel version 6.19 can cause MongoDB to crash on startup due to an incompatibility with TCMalloc.
recommend: Avoid using MongoDB 8.0 with Linux kernel version 6.19 until a patched version of TCMalloc is available.
fix: Upgrade to a different kernel version or wait for a patched version of TCMalloc.

source.title: Platform Support
source.quote: Due to an incompatibility between a new kernel release and the currently vendored version of TCMalloc, running MongoDB 8.0 or newer with Linux kernel version 6.19 can cause MongoDB to crash on startup.

threshold: has Haswell or later Core or Tiger Lake or later Celeron or Pentium
reason: MongoDB requires specific Intel processor generations to ensure compatibility and performance. Using unsupported processors may lead to suboptimal performance or failures.

source.title: Platform Support
source.quote: For Intel x86_64, MongoDB requires one of: a Haswell or later Core processor, or a Tiger Lake or later Celeron or Pentium processor.

reason: MongoDB requires specific AMD processor generations to ensure compatibility and performance. Using unsupported processors may lead to suboptimal performance or failures.

source.title: Platform Support

recommend: Verify that your Intel or AMD processor supports the AVX instruction set before deploying MongoDB 5.0 or later.
fix: Check your processor specifications for AVX support.

source.title: Platform Support
source.quote: MongoDB 5.0 requires use of the AVX instruction set, available on select Intel and AMD processors.

reason: MongoDB on arm64 requires a minimum microarchitecture to ensure compatibility and performance. Using older architectures may lead to failures or suboptimal performance.
recommend: Ensure your ARM system meets the ARMv8.2-A or later microarchitecture requirement.
fix: Check your ARM processor model and upgrade if necessary.

source.title: Platform Support
source.quote: MongoDB on arm64 requires the ARMv8.2-A or later microarchitecture.

threshold: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8.8, Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.3, SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 SP5, Amazon Linux 2023 version 2023.3
reason: Using an unsupported OS version can lead to compatibility issues and lack of support from MongoDB.
recommend: Ensure your operating system is one of the supported versions for MongoDB 8.0.
fix: Upgrade your OS to one of the supported versions listed.

source.title: Platform Support Matrix
source.quote: MongoDB 8.0 supports the following minimum OS minor versions: - Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8.8 - Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.3 - SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 SP5 - Amazon Linux 2023 version 2023.3

threshold: is one of 'Amazon Linux, Debian, RHEL, SLES, Ubuntu LTS, Windows Server'
reason: Using recommended operating systems can enhance performance and stability for MongoDB deployments.
recommend: For production use, select one of the recommended operating systems for MongoDB.
fix: Choose from Amazon Linux, Debian, SLES, Ubuntu LTS, or Windows Server for your MongoDB deployment.

source.title: Platform Support Matrix
source.quote: While MongoDB supports a variety of platforms, the following operating systems are recommended for production use on x86_64 architecture: Amazon Linux, Debian, RHEL, SLES, Ubuntu LTS, Windows Server.

reason: By default, MongoDB assumes a trusted environment and does not enable authorization, which can lead to unauthorized access if the deployment is exposed to untrusted networks.
recommend: Enable authorization mode to secure your MongoDB deployment and restrict access to authorized users only.
fix: Set 'authorization' to 'enabled' in the MongoDB configuration file or start mongod with the --auth option.

source.title: Write Concern
source.quote: By default, authorization is not enabled, and mongod assumes a trusted environment. Enable authorization mode as needed.

reason: Adjusting the connection pool size is crucial to avoid overloading the connection resources of mongod or mongos instances, which can lead to performance degradation and connection issues.
recommend: Start by setting the connection pool size to 110-115% of the typical number of current database requests and modify as necessary based on your application's needs.
fix: Adjust the connection pool size in your connection string options.

source.title: Manage Connection Pool Sizes
source.quote: Avoid overloading the connection resources of a mongod or mongos instance by adjusting the connection pool size to suit your use case. Start at 110-115% of the typical number of current database requests, and modify the connection pool size as needed.

reason: Setting the TCP keepalive time to 120 seconds helps prevent silent connection drops when the keepalive value exceeds the idle timeout of the cloud provider's load balancer.
recommend: Set tcp_keepalive_time to 120 seconds to reduce the risk of dropped connections.
fix: sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time=120

source.title: Adjust tcp_keepalive_time
source.quote: To reduce this risk, set tcp_keepalive_time to 120.

reason: The default WiredTiger internal cache size is set to optimize performance based on available RAM. Increasing it beyond the default can lead to inefficient memory usage and potential performance degradation.
recommend: Avoid increasing the WiredTiger internal cache size above its default value unless necessary. If required, use --wiredTigerCacheSizePct to specify a percentage of available memory, ensuring it does not exceed 80%.
fix: --wiredTigerCacheSizePct=<percentage>

source.title: WiredTiger
source.quote: The default WiredTiger internal cache size is the larger of either: 50% of (RAM - 1GB), or 0.256 GB. Avoid increasing the WiredTiger internal cache size above its default value. If your case requires to do so, you can use --wiredTigerCacheSizePct to account for changes in memory due to vertical.

reason: The WiredTiger internal cache size limits the amount of memory used by MongoDB for caching data. If this value is too high relative to the available RAM, it can lead to performance degradation as the system may need to swap memory or evict cached data frequently.
recommend: Decrease the WiredTiger internal cache size to accommodate other processes or MongoDB instances running on the same machine or within the same container.
fix: Set storage.wiredTiger.engineConfig.cacheSizeGB to a value less than the available RAM in the container.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Options
source.quote: To accommodate the additional consumers of RAM, you may have to decrease WiredTiger internal cache size. The default WiredTiger internal cache size value assumes that there is a single mongod instance per machine. If a single machine contains multiple MongoDB instances, decrease the setting to accommodate the other mongod instances.

reason: Running MongoDB on NUMA hardware can lead to slow performance and high system process usage due to the way memory is accessed. If the NUMA configuration is not optimal, it can degrade the performance of MongoDB significantly.
recommend: Configure a memory interleave policy to ensure the host behaves in a non-NUMA fashion when running MongoDB.
fix: Set the memory interleave policy in the system settings.

source.title: MongoDB and NUMA Hardware
source.quote: When running MongoDB servers and clients on NUMA hardware, you should configure a memory interleave policy so that the host behaves in a non-NUMA fashion. MongoDB checks NUMA settings on start up when deployed on Linux (since version 2.0) and Windows (since version 2.6) machines.

reason: The numad daemon can negatively impact the performance of MongoDB by altering memory allocation patterns. Ensuring that numad is disabled can help maintain optimal performance.
recommend: Ensure that the numad service is not enabled on MongoDB servers to avoid performance degradation.
fix: systemctl disable numad

source.title: MongoDB and NUMA Hardware
source.quote: The numad daemon process can also reduce mongod performance. You should ensure numad is not enabled on MongoDB servers.

reason: Disabling zone reclaim is crucial for optimal performance of MongoDB on NUMA hardware. Zone reclaim can lead to performance degradation due to inefficient memory allocation across NUMA nodes.
recommend: Set the zone reclaim mode to 0 to disable it, ensuring better memory management for MongoDB instances.
fix: echo 0 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/zone_reclaim_mode

source.title: Configuring NUMA on Linux
source.quote: On Linux, you must disable zone reclaim... Disable zone reclaim with one of the following commands: echo 0 | sudo tee /proc/sys/vm/zone_reclaim_mode

reason: Starting MongoDB instances with numactl is essential for proper NUMA configuration. This ensures that memory allocation is optimized across NUMA nodes, which can significantly enhance performance.
recommend: Ensure that all MongoDB instances are started using numactl to leverage NUMA optimally.
fix: numactl --interleave=all /usr/local/bin/mongod -f /etc/mongod.conf

source.title: Configuring NUMA on Linux
source.quote: Ensure that mongod and mongos instances are started by numactl... You must use numactl to start each of your mongod instances...

reason: Copying the default MongoDB service file is essential for customizing the service configuration without affecting the default installation.
recommend: Copy the default service file to the systemd directory for modifications.
fix: sudo cp /lib/systemd/system/mongod.service /etc/systemd/system/

source.title: Tip
source.quote: Copy the default MongoDB service file: sudo cp /lib/systemd/system/mongod.service /etc/systemd/system/

reason: Updating the ExecStart statement allows for better memory management by interleaving memory across NUMA nodes.
recommend: Edit the ExecStart line in the mongod.service file to include numactl for improved performance.
fix: Edit /etc/systemd/system/mongod.service and update ExecStart to: /usr/bin/numactl --interleave=all

source.title: Tip
source.quote: Edit the /etc/systemd/system/mongod.service file, and update the ExecStart statement to begin with: /usr/bin/numactl --interleave=all

reason: High swappiness values can lead to excessive swapping, which negatively impacts MongoDB performance. Keeping swappiness low ensures that the system prioritizes RAM usage over swap space, which is crucial for database operations.
recommend: Set the vm.swappiness parameter to a low value to minimize swapping and improve performance.
fix: sysctl -w vm.swappiness=10

source.title: Swap
source.quote: MongoDB performs best where swapping can be avoided or kept to a minimum, as retrieving data from swap will always be slower than accessing data in RAM.

reason: Using NFS with the correct options can help mitigate performance degradation when using remote file systems with MongoDB's WiredTiger storage engine.
recommend: Ensure that your NFS configuration in /etc/fstab includes the recommended options to optimize performance.
fix:
Add the following lines to your /etc/fstab:
- bg
- hard
- nolock
- noatime
- nointr

source.title: Remote Filesystems (NFS)
source.quote: If you decide to use NFS, add the following NFS options to your /etc/fstab file: bg, hard, nolock, noatime, nointr.

reason: Separating database components onto different storage devices can significantly improve performance by optimizing access patterns and reducing contention for I/O resources.
recommend: Consider separating your database's data, journal, and logs onto different storage devices based on your application's access and write pattern.
fix: Use symbolic links to map each component's path to the device storing it.

source.title: Separate Components onto Different Storage Devices
source.quote: For improved performance, consider separating your database's data, journal, and logs onto different storage devices, based on your application's access and write pattern.

reason: Using the none scheduler allows the operating system to defer I/O scheduling to the underlying hypervisor, which can significantly improve performance for virtual or cloud-hosted devices.
recommend: Configure the guest operating system to use the none scheduler for optimal I/O performance.
fix: Set the scheduler to 'none' in the operating system configuration.

source.title: Scheduling for Virtual or Cloud Hosted Devices
source.quote: For local block devices attached to a virtual machine instance via the hypervisor or hosted by a cloud hosting provider, the guest operating system should use the none scheduler for best performance.

reason: The mq-deadline scheduler is optimized for spinning disks, providing better throughput and lower latency for disk-intensive applications.
recommend: Configure the operating system to use the mq-deadline scheduler for physical servers with spinning disks.
fix: echo 'mq-deadline' > /sys/block/sda/queue/scheduler

source.title: Scheduling for Physical Servers
source.quote: For physical servers using spinning disks, the operating system should use the mq-deadline scheduler.

reason: WiredTiger uses the snappy compression library by default, which balances compression efficiency and CPU cost. Understanding the default setting is crucial for optimizing storage performance based on application needs.
recommend: Consider changing the compression library if your application has specific performance requirements.
fix: Set storage.wiredTiger.collectionConfig.blockCompressor to 'zlib' or 'zstd' as needed.

source.title: Compression
source.quote: By default, WiredTiger uses snappy compression library. To change the compression setting, see storage.wiredTiger.collectionConfig.blockCompressor.

reason: The maxAcceptableLogicalClockDriftSecs parameter controls the acceptable clock drift between MongoDB components. A lower value reduces tolerance for clock drift, which is critical for time-dependent operations.
recommend: Set maxAcceptableLogicalClockDriftSecs to a lower value to ensure tighter synchronization between components.
fix: Set maxAcceptableLogicalClockDriftSecs to a value less than 60 seconds.

source.title: Compression
source.quote: Clusters with a lower value of maxAcceptableLogicalClockDriftSecs have a correspondingly lower tolerance for clock drift.

reason: Using an appropriate Linux kernel version is crucial for MongoDB performance and stability. Versions below 2.6.36 may not support necessary features or optimizations.
recommend: Upgrade to at least Linux kernel version 2.6.36 or later for optimal performance.
fix: Upgrade your Linux kernel to version 2.6.36 or later.

source.title: Kernel and File Systems
source.quote: When running MongoDB in production on Linux, you should use Linux kernel version 2.6.36 or later, with either the XFS or EXT4 filesystem.

reason: XFS generally performs better with MongoDB compared to EXT4, especially with the WiredTiger storage engine.
recommend: Use the XFS filesystem for MongoDB data bearing nodes to avoid potential performance issues.
fix: Format your data partition with the XFS filesystem.

source.title: Kernel and File Systems
source.quote: If possible, use XFS as it generally performs better with MongoDB.

reason: Using the appropriate kernel version with EXT4 is necessary to ensure compatibility and performance for MongoDB.
recommend: Ensure that you are using at least Linux kernel version 2.6.28 if you are using the EXT4 filesystem.
fix: Upgrade your Linux kernel to version 2.6.28 or later if using EXT4.

source.title: Kernel and File Systems
source.quote: If you use the EXT4 file system, use at least version 2.6.28 of the Linux Kernel.

reason: Using the latest version of the GNU C Library (glibc) ensures that you benefit from the latest performance improvements and security patches, which can significantly affect the stability and efficiency of MongoDB operations.
recommend: Regularly check for updates to glibc using your system's package manager and apply them as needed.
fix: sudo yum update glibc (for RHEL/CentOS) or sudo apt-get install libc6 (for Ubuntu/Debian)

source.title: System C Library
source.quote: For best results, use the latest update available for this system-provided version. You can check whether you have the latest version installed by using your system's package manager.

reason: Swappiness influences how the Linux kernel manages memory. A lower value reduces swapping, which is beneficial for MongoDB performance.
recommend: Set vm.swappiness to 1 or 0 based on your application needs and cluster configuration.
fix: sysctl -w vm.swappiness=1

source.title: Set vm.swappiness to 1 or 0
source.quote: MongoDB performs best where swapping can be avoided or kept to a minimum. As such you should set vm.swappiness to either 1 or 0 depending on your application needs and cluster configuration.

reason: Setting vm.swappiness to 1 helps to reduce swapping, which can improve performance for MongoDB instances, especially when they are running on systems with other software.
recommend: Set vm.swappiness to 1 to minimize swapping and improve MongoDB performance.
fix: Edit /etc/sysctl.conf and add 'vm.swappiness = 1', then run 'sudo sysctl -p' to apply the changes.

source.title: MongoDB Production Notes
source.quote: If your MongoDB instance is hosted on a system that also runs other software, such as a webserver, you should set vm.swappiness to 1.

reason: Synchronizing time across hosts is crucial for MongoDB deployments, especially in sharded clusters, to ensure data consistency and avoid issues related to time discrepancies.
recommend: Ensure that NTP is configured and running on all MongoDB hosts to maintain accurate time synchronization.
fix: Install and configure NTP on all hosts.

source.title: Recommended Configuration
source.quote: Use the Network Time Protocol (NTP) to synchronize time among your hosts. This is especially important in sharded clusters.

reason: Low ulimit values can negatively affect MongoDB performance under heavy load, leading to failed connections and service loss.
recommend: Adjust the ulimit settings to ensure the number of open files is at least 64000 to avoid startup warnings and performance issues.
fix: Set ulimit -n 64000

source.title: Recommended Configuration
source.quote: If the ulimit value for number of open files is under 64000, MongoDB generates a startup warning.

threshold: is 'enabled' for MongoDB 8.0 or 'disabled' for MongoDB 7.0 or earlier
reason: The performance of MongoDB can be significantly affected by the configuration of Transparent Hugepages, depending on the version being used.
recommend: Enable Transparent Hugepages if using MongoDB 8.0, or disable it if using MongoDB 7.0 or earlier to optimize performance.
fix: echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled (for disabling) or ensure it's enabled for MongoDB 8.0

source.title: Recommended Configuration
source.quote: If you are running MongoDB 8.0, enable Transparent Hugepages. If you are running MongoDB 7.0 or earlier, disable Transparent Hugepages.

reason: If the ulimit value for the number of open files is under 64000, MongoDB generates a startup warning, which can indicate potential issues with file handling and resource limits.
recommend: Increase the ulimit value for the number of open files to at least 64000 to avoid startup warnings and ensure MongoDB operates smoothly.
fix: ulimit -n 64000

source.title: MongoDB Production Notes
source.quote: If the ulimit value for number of open files is under 64000, MongoDB generates a startup warning.

reason: For MongoDB 8.0, enabling Transparent Hugepages can improve performance, while for earlier versions, it should be disabled to optimize memory usage.
recommend: Enable Transparent Hugepages if running MongoDB 8.0; otherwise, disable it for versions 7.0 and earlier.
fix: echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled

source.title: MongoDB Production Notes
source.quote: If you are running MongoDB 8.0, enable Transparent Hugepages. If you are running MongoDB 7.0 or earlier, disable Transparent Hugepages.

reason: Setting the readahead value between 8 and 32 can optimize performance for MongoDB, as higher values may lead to performance degradation due to random disk access patterns.
recommend: Configure the readahead setting to be between 8 and 32 for optimal MongoDB performance.
fix: echo 16 > /sys/block/<device>/queue/read_ahead_kb

source.title: MongoDB Production Notes
source.quote: Set the readahead setting between 8 and 32 regardless of storage media type (spinning disk, SSD, etc.).

reason: Disabling DVFS and CPU power saving modes ensures that the CPU operates at maximum performance, which is essential for workloads that require consistent processing power.
recommend: Disable DVFS and CPU power saving modes to maintain consistent CPU performance.
fix: echo 'performance' | sudo tee /sys/class/drm/card0/device/power_dpm_state

source.title: MongoDB on Virtual Environments
source.quote: Disable DVFS and CPU power saving modes.

reason: Using numactl to bind memory locality to a single socket can improve memory access times and overall performance for MongoDB.
recommend: Utilize numactl to bind memory locality to a single socket for better performance.
fix: numactl --cpubind=0 --membind=0 <your_command>

source.title: MongoDB on Virtual Environments
source.quote: Use numactl to bind memory locality to a single socket.

reason: Reserving the full amount of memory for the virtual machine running MongoDB prevents the balloon driver from inflating, which can negatively impact MongoDB's performance due to memory pressure.
recommend: Ensure that the virtual machine running MongoDB has its memory fully reserved to avoid performance degradation.
fix: Set memory reservation for the VM in VMware settings.

source.title: MongoDB on Virtual Environments
source.quote: To prevent negative performance impact from the balloon driver and memory overcommitment features, reserve the full amount of memory for the virtual machine running MongoDB.

reason: Reserving the full amount of memory for the virtual machine running MongoDB prevents the balloon driver from inflating, which can negatively impact MongoDB's performance due to memory pressure.
recommend: Ensure that the virtual machine running MongoDB has the full amount of memory reserved to avoid performance degradation.
fix: Configure the VM to reserve the full amount of memory allocated for MongoDB.

source.title: MongoDB on Virtual Environments
source.quote: To prevent negative performance impact from the balloon driver and memory overcommitment features, reserve the full amount of memory for the virtual machine running MongoDB.

reason: Monitoring disk I/O is crucial for identifying potential bottlenecks in database performance. The %util field indicates how much time the disk is in use, which helps in assessing whether the disk is a limiting factor.
recommend: Use the iostat command to check disk I/O utilization and identify any bottlenecks.
fix: Run 'iostat -xmt 1' to get detailed I/O statistics.

source.title: Production Notes
source.quote: On Linux, use the iostat command to check if disk I/O is a bottleneck for your database. Specify a number of seconds when running iostat to avoid displaying stats covering the time since server boot.

reason: NUMA Balancing can cause performance instability due to frequent page migrations, which is detrimental for database operations.
recommend: On NUMA hosts running databases, disable kernel NUMA auto-balancing to avoid page migration jitter. (MongoDB documentation does not directly cover this; openEuler / RHEL Linux performance tuning guides recommend it for DB workloads.)
fix:
echo 0 | sudo tee /proc/sys/kernel/numa_balancing
# Persist:
echo 'kernel.numa_balancing = 0' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.d/99-mongodb.conf

source.title: openEuler Performance Tuning · NUMA balancing(supplementary; MongoDB docs do not explicitly cover this)
source.quote: (NOTE: MongoDB official documentation does not cover kernel.numa_balancing. This recommendation derives from Linux/openEuler general DB tuning guidance.)

reason: Binding MongoDB to a single NUMA node can reduce memory access latency and improve performance.
recommend: Start every mongod / mongos / config server with `numactl --interleave=all` per MongoDB Production Notes (Configuring NUMA on Linux). Do NOT use `--cpunodebind`/`--membind` for single-node binding — MongoDB official guidance is interleave-all.
fix:
# systemd · per MongoDB official 'Configuring NUMA on Linux'
ExecStart=/usr/bin/numactl --interleave=all /usr/bin/mongod --config /etc/mongod.conf

source.title: Configuring NUMA on Linux · MongoDB Production Notes
source.quote: You must use `numactl` to start each of your `mongod` instances, including all config servers, `mongos` instances, and clients. Edit the default systemd service file for each as follows: ... ExecStart=/usr/bin/numactl --interleave=all /usr/bin/mongod --config /etc/mongod.conf

reason: Using interleave mode can be beneficial in scenarios where precise CPU binding is not feasible, allowing for better memory access across NUMA nodes.
recommend: Use `numactl --interleave=all` to start mongod (single instance) per MongoDB official guidance. Per-NUMA-node sharding (one mongod per node bound to that node) is documented as an alternative for sharded deployments.
fix: numactl --interleave=all /usr/local/bin/mongod -f /etc/mongod.conf

source.title: Configuring NUMA on Linux · MongoDB Production Notes
source.quote: Configure each of your custom init scripts to start each MongoDB instance via `numactl`: numactl --interleave=all <path> <options>
