reason: If the configuration file includes an expansion directive and the mongod or mongos is started without the --configExpand option, it will fail to start. This is critical for ensuring that the server starts correctly with the intended configuration.
recommend: Always include the --configExpand option when using expansion directives in your configuration file.
fix: mongod --config /etc/mongod.conf --configExpand "rest,exec"

source.title: Expansion Directives and --configExpand
source.quote: If the configuration file includes an expansion directive and you start the mongod/mongos without specifying that directive in the --configExpand option, the mongod/mongos fails to start.

reason: Using syslog for logging can lead to misleading timestamps, especially under heavy load, which can complicate troubleshooting and monitoring efforts.
recommend: Switch to using the file option for logging to ensure accurate timestamps in log entries.
fix: Set 'systemLog.destination' to 'file' in the MongoDB configuration.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: We recommend using the file option for production systems to ensure accurate timestamps.

reason: The verbosity level for access control log messages can help in diagnosing security-related issues and understanding access control operations.
recommend: Set the verbosity level between 1 to 5 for more detailed logging of access control events.
fix: systemLog.component.accessControl.verbosity: 2

source.title: System Log Component Options
source.quote: The verbosity level can range from 0 to 5: 0 is the MongoDB's default log verbosity level, to include Informational messages. 1 to 5 increases the verbosity level to include Debug messages.

reason: The verbosity level for command log messages can provide insights into command execution and performance issues.
recommend: Consider increasing the verbosity level to 2 or higher to capture detailed command execution logs.
fix: systemLog.component.command.verbosity: 2

source.title: System Log Component Options
source.quote: The verbosity level can range from 0 to 5: 0 is the MongoDB's default log verbosity level, to include Informational messages. 1 to 5 increases the verbosity level to include Debug messages.

reason: The verbosity level for control log messages can assist in monitoring control operations and system behavior.
recommend: Adjust the verbosity level to 1 or higher to gain better visibility into control operations.
fix: systemLog.component.control.verbosity: 2

source.title: System Log Component Options
source.quote: The verbosity level can range from 0 to 5: 0 is the MongoDB's default log verbosity level, to include Informational messages. 1 to 5 increases the verbosity level to include Debug messages.

reason: The time zone database path is crucial for accurate time zone conversions in MongoDB. If not set, MongoDB uses its built-in database, which may not be up-to-date.
recommend: Set the processManagement.timeZoneInfo option to the correct path if you need a specific time zone database.
fix: processManagement.timeZoneInfo: '/usr/share/zoneinfo'

source.title: macOS
source.quote: The configuration file included with Linux and macOS packages sets the time zone database path to /usr/share/zoneinfo by default.

reason: Enabling IPv6 support is crucial for applications that require communication over IPv6 addresses. If not enabled, the application may fail to connect to services that are only accessible via IPv6, leading to potential downtime or connectivity issues.
recommend: Ensure that the MongoDB server is started with the configuration option net.ipv6 set to true to enable IPv6 support.
fix: set net.ipv6: true in the MongoDB configuration file or start mongod with the --ipv6 option.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: If specifying an IPv6 address or a hostname that resolves to an IPv6 address to net.bindIp, you must start mongos or mongod with net.ipv6 : true to enable IPv6 support.

threshold: uses DNS hostnames instead of IP addresses
reason: Using DNS hostnames instead of IP addresses for configuring replica set members or sharded cluster members is crucial to avoid configuration updates due to IP address changes. Starting from MongoDB 5.0, nodes configured only with an IP address will fail startup validation and will not start, leading to potential downtime.
recommend: Always configure MongoDB nodes with DNS hostnames to ensure proper startup and avoid issues related to IP address changes.
fix: Update your MongoDB configuration to use DNS hostnames for all nodes.

source.title: Important
source.quote: Use DNS hostnames instead of IP addresses. It is particularly important to use a DNS hostname instead of an IP address when configuring replica set members or sharded cluster members. Starting in MongoDB 5.0, nodes that are only configured with an IP address fail startup validation and do not start.

reason: Enabling authentication is crucial for securing your MongoDB cluster from unauthorized access. Without it, your database could be exposed to potential threats and attacks.
recommend: Review the security checklist and ensure that authentication is enabled for your MongoDB deployment.
fix: Refer to the Security Checklist for Self-Managed Deployments documentation to enable authentication.

source.title: Security Checklist for Self-Managed Deployments
source.quote: At minimum, consider enabling authentication and hardening network infrastructure.

reason: Binding to all IP addresses can expose your MongoDB instance to the public internet, which increases the risk of unauthorized access. It's important to configure this setting carefully to ensure security.
recommend: Consider using net.bindIpAll to bind to all IPv4 and IPv6 addresses only if necessary, and ensure proper security measures are in place.
fix: Set net.bindIpAll to true in your MongoDB configuration file.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Options
source.quote: If true, the mongos or mongod instance binds to all IPv4 addresses (i.e. 0.0.0.0).

reason: Starting in MongoDB v5.0, using IP addresses instead of hostnames for replica set configurations is rejected. This can lead to issues when configuring or reconfiguring replica sets.
recommend: Use hostnames instead of IP addresses when configuring replica sets to avoid potential errors.
fix: Update your replica set configuration to use hostnames instead of IP addresses.

source.title: Replica Set Initiate
source.quote: Starting in MongoDB v5.0, replSetInitiate and replSetReconfig reject configurations that use IP addresses instead of hostnames.

reason: Binding MongoDB to a publicly-accessible IP address without proper security measures can expose your database to unauthorized access, leading to potential data breaches and security vulnerabilities.
recommend: Ensure that your MongoDB instance is secured by enabling authentication and hardening your network infrastructure before binding to a public IP.
fix: Set net.bindIp to '::,0.0.0.0' or '*' in your MongoDB configuration file.

source.title: IP Binding in Self-Managed Deployments
source.quote: Alternatively, set net.bindIp to ::,0.0.0.0 or to an asterisk '*' (enclose the asterisk in quotes to distinguish from YAML alias nodes) to bind to all IP addresses.

reason: Setting net.maxIncomingConnections too high can lead to MongoDB using default values, which may not meet application needs, especially under high load.
recommend: Ensure net.maxIncomingConnections is set to a value less than or equal to (RLIMIT_NOFILE / 2) * 0.8 to avoid automatic fallback to defaults.
fix: Set net.maxIncomingConnections to an appropriate value based on your system's RLIMIT_NOFILE setting.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Options
source.quote: On Linux, net.maxIncomingConnections must be less than or equal to the value of (RLIMIT_NOFILE / 2) * 0.8. If you try to set a larger value, MongoDB automatically uses the default.

reason: Enabling net.wireObjectCheck helps prevent malformed BSON from being inserted into the database, which is crucial for data integrity.
recommend: Keep net.wireObjectCheck enabled to ensure that all requests from clients are validated.
fix: Set net.wireObjectCheck to true in your MongoDB configuration.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Options
source.quote: When true, the mongod or mongos instance validates all requests from clients upon receipt to prevent clients from inserting malformed or invalid BSON into a MongoDB database.

reason: Enabling IPv6 support is essential for modern applications that require compatibility with IPv6 networks.
recommend: Set net.ipv6 to true to enable IPv6 support in MongoDB.
fix: Set net.ipv6 to true in your MongoDB configuration.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Options
source.quote: Set net.ipv6 to true to enable IPv6 support. mongos/mongod disables IPv6 support by default.

reason: Disabling the UNIX domain socket can limit local communication between MongoDB processes, which may impact performance and functionality.
recommend: Ensure that net.unixDomainSocket.enabled is set to true to allow local connections via UNIX sockets.
fix: set net.unixDomainSocket.enabled: true in the configuration file

source.title: net.unixDomainSocket
source.quote: Enable or disable listening on the UNIX domain socket. net.unixDomainSocket.enabled applies only to Unix-based systems.

reason: The default path for the UNIX socket is /tmp, which may not be suitable for all environments due to security or organizational policies.
recommend: Consider changing net.unixDomainSocket.pathPrefix to a more secure or appropriate directory for your environment.
fix: set net.unixDomainSocket.pathPrefix: '/your/custom/path' in the configuration file

source.title: net.unixDomainSocket
source.quote: The path for the UNIX socket. net.unixDomainSocket.pathPrefix applies only to Unix-based systems.

reason: Using 'requireTLS' ensures that all connections to the MongoDB server are encrypted, which is critical for protecting sensitive data during transmission.
recommend: Set the net.tls.mode to 'requireTLS' to enforce encrypted connections.
fix: net.tls.mode: requireTLS

source.title: TLS Options
source.quote: The server uses and accepts only TLS encrypted connections.

reason: The certificateKeyFile is required for TLS to function properly on Linux/BSD, ensuring secure communication.
recommend: Specify the net.tls.certificateKeyFile to enable TLS on Linux/BSD.
fix: net.tls.certificateKeyFile: /path/to/certificate.pem

source.title: TLS Options
source.quote: On Linux/BSD, you must specify net.tls.certificateKeyFile when TLS is enabled.

reason: On Windows, using a certificateSelector is necessary to avoid issues with encrypted PEM files, which are not supported.
recommend: Use net.tls.certificateSelector on Windows to specify a certificate from the secure certificate store.
fix: net.tls.certificateSelector: 'your-certificate-selector'

source.title: TLS Options
source.quote: On Windows only, MongoDB does not support encrypted PEM files.

reason: Using net.tls.certificateSelector or net.tls.clusterCertificateSelector is not compatible with net.tls.CAFile or net.tls.clusterCAFile, which can lead to misconfiguration of TLS settings.
recommend: Avoid using net.tls.CAFile or net.tls.clusterCAFile when net.tls.certificateSelector or net.tls.clusterCertificateSelector is in use.
fix: Remove net.tls.CAFile and net.tls.clusterCAFile from the configuration if net.tls.certificateSelector or net.tls.clusterCertificateSelector is set.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: If you use net.tls.certificateSelector and/or net.tls.clusterCertificateSelector, we do not recommend using net.tls.CAFile or net.tls.clusterCAFile to specify the root and intermediate CA certificate.

reason: Logging TLS versions helps in monitoring and debugging secure connections to the MongoDB server, ensuring that clients are using secure protocols.
recommend: Set the net.tls.logVersions configuration option to 'TLS1_2,TLS1_3' to enable logging of these TLS versions.
fix: net.tls.logVersions: 'TLS1_2,TLS1_3'

source.title: Example
source.quote: To instruct mongos or mongod to log a message when a client connects using either TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3, set net.tls.logVersions to 'TLS1_2,TLS1_3'.

reason: The default compressors for MongoDB communication can significantly impact performance and bandwidth usage. Using efficient compression algorithms can reduce the amount of data transmitted over the network, improving overall performance.
recommend: Consider using the default compressors snappy, zstd, and zlib for optimal performance in network communication.
fix: Set net.compression.compressors to 'snappy,zstd,zlib' in the MongoDB configuration file.

source.title: Configuration Options
source.quote: Default: snappy,zstd,zlib Specifies the default compressor(s) to use for communication between this mongod or mongos instance and other members of the deployment if the instance is part of a replica set or a sharded cluster.

reason: Enabling encryption is crucial for securing data at rest in the WiredTiger storage engine. If not enabled, sensitive data may be exposed to unauthorized access.
recommend: Set the 'enableEncryption' option to true to ensure that encryption is applied to your data.
fix: security.enableEncryption: true

source.title: Key Management Configuration Options
source.quote: Enables encryption for the WiredTiger storage engine. You must set to true to pass in encryption keys and configurations.

reason: Using AES256-GCM provides better performance and security features compared to AES256-CBC. However, it is only supported on Linux and not on Windows.
recommend: If using MongoDB Enterprise on Linux, consider switching to AES256-GCM for encryption at rest.
fix: Set security.encryptionCipherMode to 'AES256-GCM' in your MongoDB configuration.

source.title: Example > Enterprise Feature
source.quote: Available only on Linux. MongoDB Enterprise on Windows no longer supports AES256-GCM as a block cipher for encryption at rest. This usage is only supported on Linux.

reason: Enabling encryption is crucial for protecting sensitive data at rest in MongoDB. If encryption is not enabled, data can be exposed to unauthorized access.
recommend: Ensure that the security.enableEncryption option is set to true to protect your data.
fix: Set security.enableEncryption: true in your MongoDB configuration file.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: Requires security.enableEncryption to be true.

threshold: is set when managing keys through a process other than KMIP
reason: The encryption key file is necessary for managing encryption keys outside of KMIP. Failing to set this correctly can lead to errors when accessing encrypted data.
recommend: Set the security.encryptionKeyFile to the correct path if you are managing keys outside of KMIP.
fix: Set security.encryptionKeyFile: <path_to_keyfile> in your MongoDB configuration file.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: The path to the local keyfile when managing keys through a process other than KMIP.

threshold: is a valid hostname or IP address
reason: The KMIP server name must match the certificate presented by the KMIP server to ensure secure connections. If it does not match, mongod will not start, which can lead to service outages.
recommend: Ensure that the server name specified in security.kmip.serverName matches the SAN or CN on the KMIP server's certificate.
fix: Set security.kmip.serverName to a valid hostname or IP address that matches the KMIP server's certificate.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: The server name specified in security.kmip.serverName must match either the Subject Alternative Name SAN or the Common Name CN on the certificate presented by the KMIP server.

reason: The connectTimeoutMS setting determines how long the mongod will wait for a response from the KMIP server. If this value is too low, it may lead to connection issues, especially in environments with high latency.
recommend: Set security.kmip.connectTimeoutMS to a value of at least 1000 milliseconds to ensure reliable connections to the KMIP server.
fix: security.kmip.connectTimeoutMS: 1000

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: Timeout in milliseconds to wait for a response from the KMIP server. If the connectRetries setting is specified, the mongod waits up to the value specified with connectTimeoutMS for each retry.

reason: If security.kmip.activateKeys is false, the mongod node will fail to start if there are existing keys on the KMIP server that are not activated. This setting is crucial for the proper functioning of KMIP key management.
recommend: Ensure that security.kmip.activateKeys is set to true to activate newly created KMIP keys and maintain the operational status of the mongod node.
fix: security.kmip.activateKeys: true

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: Activates all newly created KMIP keys upon creation and then periodically checks those keys are in an active state.

reason: The keyStatePollingSeconds setting determines how frequently the mongod polls the KMIP server for active keys. A value of 900 seconds is the default, but it can be adjusted based on operational needs.
recommend: Consider adjusting security.kmip.keyStatePollingSeconds based on your environment's requirements for key state updates.
fix: security.kmip.keyStatePollingSeconds: 900

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: Frequency in seconds at which mongod polls the KMIP server for active keys. To disable polling, set the value to -1.

threshold: is a fully qualified server domain name
reason: Configuring the SASL hostname is crucial for proper SASL and Kerberos authentication. An incorrect hostname can lead to authentication failures.
recommend: Ensure that the SASL hostname is set correctly to a fully qualified domain name.
fix: Set the hostname in the configuration file: security.sasl.hostName: <your_fully_qualified_domain_name>

source.title: SASL Options
source.quote: A fully qualified server domain name for the purpose of configuring SASL and Kerberos authentication. The SASL hostname overrides the hostname only for the configuration of SASL and Kerberos.

reason: The service name for SASL can affect how authentication is processed. Using a custom service name requires ensuring that all clients are configured to use it.
recommend: Set the service name if you need to override the default 'mongodb' service name.
fix: Set the service name in the configuration file: security.sasl.serviceName: <your_service_name>

source.title: SASL Options
source.quote: Registered name of the service using SASL. This option allows you to override the default service name component of the Kerberos principal name, on a per-instance basis.

threshold: is a valid UNIX domain socket path
reason: The path to the saslauthd socket is essential for SASL authentication to function correctly. An incorrect path can lead to authentication failures.
recommend: Ensure that the saslauthd socket path is correctly configured.
fix: Set the socket path in the configuration file: security.sasl.saslauthdSocketPath: <your_socket_path>

source.title: SASL Options
source.quote: The path to the UNIX domain socket file for saslauthd.

reason: Using TLS/SSL for LDAP connections ensures that data transmitted between MongoDB and the LDAP server is encrypted, protecting sensitive information from eavesdropping.
recommend: Ensure that the transportSecurity setting is configured to 'tls' to maintain secure connections to the LDAP server.
fix: Set security.ldap.transportSecurity to 'tls' in the MongoDB configuration.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: By default, mongod or mongos creates a TLS/SSL secured connection to the LDAP server.

reason: The default timeout for LDAP server responses is set to 10000 milliseconds. Adjusting this value can help manage connection stability and responsiveness.
recommend: Consider adjusting the timeoutMS setting based on your network conditions and LDAP server response times.
fix: Set security.ldap.timeoutMS to a suitable value in the MongoDB configuration.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: The amount of time in milliseconds mongod or mongos should wait for an LDAP server to respond to a request.

reason: The retryCount setting allows the LDAP manager to retry operations after a network error, which can improve reliability in unstable network conditions.
recommend: Consider increasing the retryCount to handle transient network issues more effectively.
fix: Set security.ldap.retryCount to a desired value in the MongoDB configuration.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: Number of operation retries by the server LDAP manager after a network error.

reason: Starting in MongoDB 5.0, providing an empty string or empty array to userToDNMapping allows MongoDB to map the authenticated username as the LDAP DN, which simplifies the configuration process.
recommend: Use an empty string or empty array for userToDNMapping if you want to map the authenticated username directly as the LDAP DN.
fix: set userToDNMapping = ''

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: Starting in MongoDB 5.0, userToDNMapping accepts an empty string "" or empty array [ ] in place of a mapping document. If providing an empty string or empty array to userToDNMapping, MongoDB maps the authenticated username as the LDAP DN.

reason: The ldapAuthzQueryTemplate parameter is essential for authorizing users using LDAP. If it is unset, the mongod instance cannot perform user authorization, which can lead to security vulnerabilities.
recommend: Ensure that the ldapAuthzQueryTemplate parameter is configured in the MongoDB configuration file before starting the mongod instance.
fix: Set security.ldap.authz.queryTemplate in the configuration file.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: If unset, mongod cannot authorize users using LDAP.

audit_note: URL likely should point to configuration-options or parameters page where ldapAuthzQueryTemplate is documented

reason: Setting validateLDAPServerConfig to true ensures that the mongod or mongos instance checks the availability of the LDAP server at startup. This is crucial for ensuring that the application can authenticate users properly.
recommend: Set security.ldap.validateLDAPServerConfig to true in the configuration to enforce LDAP server availability checks during startup.
fix: Set security.ldap.validateLDAPServerConfig=true in the configuration file.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: A flag that determines if the mongod or mongos instance checks the availability of the LDAP server(s) as part of its startup.

audit_note: URL likely should point to configuration-options or parameters page

reason: The ldapUserCacheInvalidationInterval controls how often the external user cache is flushed. A longer interval reduces the load on the LDAP server but increases the risk of stale authorization data.
recommend: Consider adjusting the ldapUserCacheInvalidationInterval based on your application's tolerance for stale data versus the load on your LDAP server.
fix: setParameter: ldapUserCacheInvalidationInterval: <desired_value>

source.title: setParameter LDAP Options
source.quote: The interval (in seconds) mongod waits between external user cache flushes. After mongod flushes the external user cache, MongoDB reacquires authorization data from the LDAP server the next time an LDAP-authorized user issues an operation.

reason: The search index management host and port configuration is crucial for connecting to the index management server. It ensures that the MongoDB instance can communicate with the appropriate server for index management tasks.
recommend: Set the searchIndexManagementHostAndPort parameter to the correct host and port for your index management server.
fix: setParameter: searchIndexManagementHostAndPort: localhost:27028

source.title: Example
source.quote: setParameter: searchIndexManagementHostAndPort: localhost:27028

reason: Setting skipAuthenticationToSearchIndexManagementServer to false enhances security by ensuring that authentication is required for accessing the index management server, preventing unauthorized access.
recommend: Set the parameter skipAuthenticationToSearchIndexManagementServer to false to improve security.
fix: setParameter: skipAuthenticationToSearchIndexManagementServer: false

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: As a security best practice, we recommend setting this parameter to false.

reason: The default data storage path for MongoDB is platform-specific. Knowing the default helps in understanding where data is stored and ensuring proper backups and configurations.
recommend: Ensure that the storage.dbPath is correctly set according to your platform's default or your custom requirements.
fix: Set storage.dbPath in the configuration file to your desired path.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: The default mongod.conf configuration file included with package manager installations uses the following platform-specific default values for storage.dbPath: /data/db on Linux and macOS, \data\db on Windows.

reason: The default journal commit interval affects the durability of the journal and disk performance. Understanding this helps in tuning for performance versus durability.
recommend: Consider adjusting the commit interval based on your application's durability requirements and disk performance capabilities.
fix: Set storage.journal.commitIntervalMs to your desired value between 1 and 500 milliseconds.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: The maximum amount of time in milliseconds that the mongod process allows between journal operations is 100 milliseconds by default. Lower values increase the durability of the journal, at the expense of disk performance.

reason: The WiredTiger internal cache size is crucial for performance, as it determines how much data can be cached in memory. If set too high, it may lead to memory pressure and affect other processes.
recommend: Keep the WiredTiger internal cache size within the default limits unless absolutely necessary. If adjustments are needed, ensure it does not exceed 10000GB.
fix: Set storage.wiredTiger.engineConfig.cacheSizeGB to a value between 0.256GB and 10000GB.

source.title: WiredTiger Options
source.quote: The default WiredTiger internal cache size is the larger of either: 50% of (RAM - 1GB), or 0.256 GB. Avoid increasing the WiredTiger internal cache size above its default value.

reason: Using a percentage for the WiredTiger cache size allows for dynamic adjustment based on available memory, but it should not exceed 80% to prevent memory exhaustion.
recommend: If you need to adjust the WiredTiger cache size, use storage.wiredTiger.engineConfig.cacheSizePct to specify a percentage of available memory, ensuring it does not exceed 80%.
fix: Set storage.wiredTiger.engineConfig.cacheSizePct to a value between 0 and 80.

source.title: WiredTiger Options
source.quote: If your use case requires to do so, you can use storage.wiredTiger.engineConfig.cacheSizePct to specify a percentage of up to 80% of available memory.

reason: The in-memory storage engine can use a maximum of 10TB of memory, which is crucial for performance and capacity planning.
recommend: Ensure that the inMemorySizeGB is set appropriately based on your workload and available resources.
fix: Set 'storage.inMemory.engineConfig.inMemorySizeGB' to a value between 256MB and 10TB.

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Options
source.quote: Maximum amount of memory to allocate for in-memory storage engine data, including indexes, oplog if the mongod is part of replica set, replica set or sharded cluster metadata, etc.

reason: The default slow operation threshold is set to 100 milliseconds. Operations exceeding this threshold are considered slow and logged, which can help identify performance issues.
recommend: Consider adjusting the slowOpThresholdMs to a lower value to capture more slow operations if performance issues are suspected.
fix: Set operationProfiling.slowOpThresholdMs to a desired value (e.g., 50).

source.title: Operation Profiling Options
source.quote: The slow operation time threshold, in milliseconds. Operations that run for longer than this threshold are considered slow. The default is 100 milliseconds.

reason: The slowOpSampleRate determines the fraction of slow operations that are logged. A value of 1.0 means all slow operations are logged, while a lower value reduces the logging frequency.
recommend: Adjust the slowOpSampleRate based on the volume of slow operations and the desired logging overhead.
fix: Set operationProfiling.slowOpSampleRate to a value between 0 and 1 (e.g., 0.5).

source.title: Operation Profiling Options
source.quote: The fraction of slow operations that should be profiled or logged. operationProfiling.slowOpSampleRate accepts values between 0 and 1, inclusive.

reason: Using zstd for audit log compression can significantly reduce the size of the logs, which is important for storage efficiency and performance.
recommend: Set auditLog.compressionMode to 'zstd' to enable compression for audit logs.
fix: auditLog.compressionMode: 'zstd'

source.title: MongoDB Configuration Reference
source.quote: Specifies the compression mode for audit log encryption. You must also enable audit log encryption using either auditLog.auditEncryptionKeyIdentifier or auditLog.localAuditKeyFile.

reason: Setting a fixed slow query threshold can help in identifying slow queries consistently, but setting it too low may lead to unnecessary alerts and noise.
recommend: Consider using the default dynamic adjustment unless you have specific needs for a fixed threshold.
fix: Set the slow query threshold to 100 milliseconds in the Atlas settings.

source.title: Monitor and Improve Slow Queries with the Performance Advisor
source.quote: By default, Atlas dynamically adjusts your slow query threshold based on the execution time of operations across your cluster. However, you can opt out of this feature and instead use a fixed slow query threshold of 100 milliseconds.

reason: In a MongoDB replica set, all members must have the same replSetName for proper communication and functionality. Inconsistent names can lead to replication issues and data inconsistency.
recommend: Ensure that the replSetName is set to the same value across all members of the replica set.
fix: Set the replSetName in the configuration file: replication: replSetName: set0

source.title: Replication Configuration
source.quote: Replica set configuration is straightforward, and only requires that the replSetName have a value that is consistent among all members of the set.

reason: Enabling authentication for a MongoDB replica set using keyfiles is crucial for securing communication between members. Without this, unauthorized access may occur.
recommend: Add the keyFile option to the replica set configuration to enable authentication.
fix: Add the following to the configuration: security: keyFile: /srv/mongodb/keyfile

source.title: Replica Set Security
source.quote: To enable authentication for the replica set using keyfiles, add the following keyFile option: security: keyFile: /srv/mongodb/keyfile.

threshold: is deployed as a replica set
reason: Config servers in a sharded cluster must be deployed as a replica set to ensure high availability and reliability of the metadata stored in the config servers.
recommend: Ensure that config servers are configured as a replica set by setting the clusterRole to configsvr in the configuration file.
fix: Set the following in the configuration file: sharding: clusterRole: configsvr

source.title: Sharding Configuration
source.quote: Config servers must be deployed as a replica set. sharding: clusterRole: configsvr

reason: The slow operation threshold determines which queries are considered slow for logging and profiling. A lower threshold can help identify performance issues more effectively.
recommend: Consider lowering the slow operation threshold to capture more queries that may be impacting performance.
fix: Set operationProfiling.slowOpThresholdMs to a lower value in the MongoDB configuration file.

source.title: Configuration and Maintenance
source.quote: operationProfiling.slowOpThresholdMs configures the threshold which determines whether a query is 'slow' for the purpose of the logging system and the profiler. The default value is 100 milliseconds. Set to a lower value if the logging system and the database profiler do not return useful results or set to a higher value to only log the longest running queries.

reason: Running MongoDB 8.0 or newer with Linux kernel version 6.19 can cause MongoDB to crash on startup due to incompatibility with TCMalloc.
recommend: Avoid using Linux kernel version 6.19 with MongoDB 8.0 or newer until a patched version of TCMalloc is available.
fix: Upgrade to a compatible kernel version before starting MongoDB 8.0 or newer.

source.title: Production Notes for Self-Managed Deployments
source.quote: MongoDB 8.0 Incompatible with Kernel 6.19 Due to an incompatibility between a new kernel release and the currently vendored version of TCMalloc, running MongoDB 8.0 or newer with Linux kernel version 6.19 can cause MongoDB to crash on startup.

reason: Starting in MongoDB 8.0, new MongoDB Server versions support the minimum operating system minor version defined by the OS vendor. Using unsupported OS versions can lead to security vulnerabilities and lack of support for new features.
recommend: Ensure that your MongoDB version is at least 8.0 and that your operating system is on a supported minor version.
fix: Upgrade to MongoDB 8.0 or later and check OS compatibility.

source.title: Platform Support Matrix
source.quote: Starting in MongoDB 8.0, new MongoDB Server versions (major and minor) support the minimum operating system (OS) minor version defined by the OS vendor.

reason: Adjusting the connection pool size helps prevent overloading the connection resources of a mongod or mongos instance, which can lead to performance degradation and connection failures.
recommend: Start with a connection pool size of 110-115% of the typical number of current database requests and modify as needed based on your application's requirements.
fix: Set the connection pool size in your MongoDB configuration file or connection string.

source.title: Production Notes
source.quote: Avoid overloading the connection resources of a mongod or mongos instance by adjusting the connection pool size to suit your use case. Start at 110-115% of the typical number of current database requests, and modify the connection pool size as needed.

reason: Insufficient disk space during index builds can lead to failures, which may disrupt database operations and affect application performance.
recommend: Set the indexBuildMinAvailableDiskSpaceMB parameter to ensure there is enough disk space for index builds.
fix: set indexBuildMinAvailableDiskSpaceMB=<desired_value>

source.title: Index Builds on Populated Collections
source.quote: You can also set the minimum available disk space required for index builds using the new indexBuildMinAvailableDiskSpaceMB parameter, which stops index builds if disk space is too low.

reason: The default limit for concurrent index builds is set to three. Exceeding this limit can block additional index builds, potentially delaying index creation and impacting application performance.
recommend: Consider adjusting the maxNumActiveUserIndexBuilds parameter if you need to allow more concurrent index builds.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setParameter: 1, maxNumActiveUserIndexBuilds: <desired_value> })

source.title: Index Creation
source.quote: By default, the server allows up to three concurrent index builds. To change the number of allowed concurrent index builds, modify the maxNumActiveUserIndexBuilds parameter.

reason: The default memory limit for index builds is 200 megabytes per createIndexes command. Insufficient memory can lead to performance degradation during index creation.
recommend: Increase the maxIndexBuildMemoryUsageMegabytes parameter if you are building many indexes or working with large datasets.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setParameter: 1, maxIndexBuildMemoryUsageMegabytes: <desired_value> })

source.title: Index Creation
source.quote: Adjust the memory limit with the maxIndexBuildMemoryUsageMegabytes parameter. Increasing this parameter is only necessary in rare cases, such as when you run many simultaneous index builds with a single createIndexes command or when you index a data set larger than 500GB.

reason: Building indexes during heavy write operations can significantly impact performance and lead to longer index build times.
recommend: Designate a maintenance window to stop or reduce write operations before starting index builds.
fix: Schedule maintenance window and pause write operations before index creation.

source.title: Index Creation
source.quote: Consider designating a maintenance window during which applications stop or reduce write operations against the collection. Start the index build during this maintenance window to mitigate the potential negative impact of the build process.

reason: To perform simultaneous index builds across replica set members, each mongod must have the featureCompatibilityVersion set to at least 4.4. Failing to meet this requirement can prevent index builds from occurring as expected.
recommend: Ensure that all mongod instances in the replica set or sharded cluster have featureCompatibilityVersion set to at least 4.4.
fix: db.adminCommand({ setFeatureCompatibilityVersion: '4.4' })

source.title: Index Builds in Replicated Environments
source.quote: Each mongod in the replica set or sharded cluster must have featureCompatibilityVersion set to at least 4.4 to start index builds simultaneously across replica set members.

reason: Setting a wait time for authentication failures can help mitigate brute-force attacks by making them more time-consuming. However, it can also lead to resource consumption issues if many clients are denied access simultaneously.
recommend: Consider setting the authentication failure wait time to a value greater than 0 to enhance security against brute-force attacks.
fix: mongod --setParameter authenticationFailureWaitTime=1000

source.title: MongoDB Manual Reference Parameters
source.quote: The number of milliseconds to wait before informing clients that their authentication attempt has failed. This parameter may be in the range 0 to 5000, inclusive.

reason: The maximum number of connections to keep open to each LDAP server is crucial for maintaining efficient communication and resource utilization. Setting this value appropriately can prevent connection exhaustion and ensure that the application can handle the required load without delays.
recommend: Ensure that the maximum connections per host is set to 2147483647 to allow for sufficient connections to LDAP servers.
fix: setParameter ldapConnectionPoolMaximumConnectionsPerHost=2147483647

source.title: MongoDB Manual Reference Parameters
source.quote: ldapConnectionPoolMaximumConnectionsPerHostAvailable for mongod only. Changed starting in MongoDB versions 5.0.9 and 6.0.0 Changed default value to 2147483647. In previous versions, the default is unset. Default: 2147483647 The maximum number of connections to keep open to each LDAP server.

reason: The idle timeout for LDAP connections is important to manage resource usage effectively. If connections remain idle for too long, they can consume resources unnecessarily, leading to potential performance issues.
recommend: Set the idle timeout for LDAP connections to 300 seconds to ensure that idle connections are closed promptly, freeing up resources.
fix: setParameter ldapConnectionPoolIdleHostTimeoutSecs=300

source.title: MongoDB Manual Reference Parameters
source.quote: ldapConnectionPoolIdleHostTimeoutSecs Available for mongod only. Default: 300 The maximum number of seconds that the pooled connections to an LDAP server can remain idle before being closed.

reason: Maintaining a minimum number of connections to LDAP servers ensures that the application can quickly respond to authentication requests without the overhead of establishing new connections each time.
recommend: Set the minimum connections per host to 1 to ensure that at least one connection is always available for immediate use.
fix: setParameter ldapConnectionPoolMinimumConnectionsPerHost=1

source.title: MongoDB Manual Reference Parameters
source.quote: ldapConnectionPoolMinimumConnectionsPerHost Available for mongod only. Default: 1 The minimum number of connections to keep open to each LDAP server.

reason: Setting the ldapRetryCount parameter allows the server to retry LDAP operations after a network error, which can improve reliability in LDAP-dependent deployments.
recommend: Consider setting ldapRetryCount to a value greater than 0 to enhance the resilience of LDAP operations.
fix: mongod --ldapRetryCount=3

source.title: LDAP Configuration Parameters
source.quote: For MongoDB deployments using LDAP Authorization on Self-Managed Deployments. Number of operation retries by the server LDAP manager after a network error.

reason: The ldapUserCacheInvalidationInterval determines how often the external user cache is flushed and refreshed with data from the LDAP server. A longer interval can reduce the frequency of updates but may lead to stale authorization data if users change their permissions frequently.
recommend: Consider adjusting the ldapUserCacheInvalidationInterval based on your deployment's needs for user authorization freshness versus performance.
fix: Set ldapUserCacheInvalidationInterval to a desired value during MongoDB startup.

source.title: LDAP Configuration
source.quote: The interval (in seconds) that the mongod instance waits between external user cache flushes. After MongoDB flushes the external user cache, MongoDB reacquires authorization data from the LDAP server the next time an LDAP-authorized user issues an operation.
